agent_output: {"handoff_brief":{"recipient":"Shaq","task_context":"Implement a user authentication feature with session management (sliding 30-minute expiration) and account suspension enforcement. Bird defined the domain rules and acceptance criteria; MJ defined the technical architecture using Redis-backed tokens. There is a terminology conflict between Bird and MJ on account status naming that must be resolved before implementing the account-status check — see contradictions_resolved and escalations.","domain_rules":[{"rule":"A session is valid for 30 minutes from the last user activity (sliding window)","testable_assertion":"Any authenticated request resets the session expiry clock to now + 30 minutes; if no request arrives within 30 minutes the session is invalid"},{"rule":"A suspended account cannot initiate a new session","testable_assertion":"An account with suspended status receives a 403 ACCOUNT_SUSPENDED on any login attempt; no token/session is created"}],"architecture_guidance":[{"decision":"Session state is implemented as a Redis-stored token with a sliding TTL of 1800 seconds","implementation_note":"On each authenticated request that passes validation, reset the Redis TTL to 1800s. This implements the 30-minute sliding window without needing a separate last_accessed_at update — Redis TTL IS the expiry clock."},{"decision":"Token validation runs as middleware on all authenticated routes","implementation_note":"Middleware checks Redis for token existence. If the key has expired (missing from Redis), return 401 SESSION_EXPIRED. Do NOT catch and translate Redis miss as a generic 401 — use the specific error code."},{"decision":"Account status check happens at token issuance (login), not on every request","implementation_note":"At login, query the account's status. If the account is suspended/locked (TERMINOLOGY UNRESOLVED — see contradictions_resolved), reject with 403 ACCOUNT_SUSPENDED before creating any token. This keeps per-request middleware lightweight."}],"acceptance_criteria":[{"criterion":"Expired session returns 401 SESSION_EXPIRED","given":"An active session that has been idle for 31 minutes (no requests made)","when":"Any authenticated request is made using that session's token","then":"The server returns HTTP 401 with error code SESSION_EXPIRED and no session state is revived"},{"criterion":"Suspended account cannot log in","given":"An account with a suspended status in the account store","when":"A login attempt is made with valid credentials for that account","then":"The server returns HTTP 403 with error code ACCOUNT_SUSPENDED and no token is issued"}],"terminology_alignment":[{"domain_term":"session","technical_term":"token (Redis key with TTL)","definition":"A session in the domain maps to a Redis-stored token. The token's existence and TTL in Redis represents the session's validity and sliding expiration window."},{"domain_term":"last user activity","technical_term":"Redis TTL reset","definition":"Each authenticated request resets the Redis key's TTL to 1800s, which is functionally equivalent to updating a last_accessed_at timestamp."},{"domain_term":"suspended account","technical_term":"UNRESOLVED — MJ used 'locked'","definition":"CONFLICT: Bird's domain model uses 'suspended account' while MJ's architecture references 'locked' status. These may be the same concept or distinct states. Do NOT implement until aligned. See escalation."}],"contradictions_resolved":[{"conflict":"TERMINOLOGY CONFLICT (UNRESOLVED): Bird defines the domain concept as 'suspended account' (status: suspended). MJ's architecture references accounts with status 'locked'. It is ambiguous whether these are the same state with inconsistent naming, or two distinct account states with different business meanings.","resolution":"Escalated to Bird for authoritative terminology. Shaq MUST NOT implement the account-status check until Bird confirms: (a) the canonical status name, and (b) whether 'suspended' and 'locked' are synonyms or distinct states. All other work (Redis token lifecycle, middleware, session expiry) can proceed in parallel."}],"open_questions":["Is 'suspended' the only non-active account status, or are there others (e.g., locked, deactivated, banned) that also block login?","Should an account suspended DURING an active session have its existing sessions immediately invalidated, or only be blocked from new sessions?","What is the token format — opaque random string or structured (e.g., JWT with Redis as revocation store)?"]},"escalations":[{"type":"terminology_conflict","description":"Bird uses 'suspended account' as the domain term for accounts that cannot initiate sessions. MJ's architecture uses 'locked' as the account status that triggers a 403 at token issuance. These terms are not aligned and could refer to the same concept or to different account states.","agents_involved":["Bird","MJ"],"routed_to":"Bird","options":["Option A: 'suspended' is the canonical domain term; MJ should update architecture to use 'suspended' status","Option B: 'suspended' and 'locked' are distinct states with different business rules; Bird should define both","Option C: Both terms map to a single 'inactive' parent status with sub-types"],"recommendation":"Option A is most likely — this appears to be a naming inconsistency rather than a genuine domain distinction. Bird should confirm 'suspended' as canonical and MJ should update the architecture doc."}],"confidence":{"level":45,"high_confidence_areas":["Session sliding window implementation via Redis TTL is well-aligned between Bird and MJ","AC1 (session expiry) is fully specified and implementable now","HTTP status codes (401 for expired session, 403 for blocked account) are agreed"],"low_confidence_areas":["Account status terminology — 'suspended' vs 'locked' is unresolved","Whether account suspension should retroactively kill active sessions","Whether other non-active account states exist beyond suspended/locked"],"assumptions":["Bird and MJ intended 'suspended' and 'locked' to mean the same thing (naming drift, not a real domain distinction)","The 30-minute window is always sliding (reset on activity), never fixed from login time","Redis is already available in the deployment environment"]}}
agent_output_excerpt: {"handoff_brief":{"recipient":"Shaq","task_context":"Implement a user authentication feature with session management (sliding 30-minute expiration) and account suspension enforcement. Bird defined the domain rules and acceptance criteria; MJ defined the technical architecture using Redis-backed tokens. There is a terminology conflict between Bird and MJ on account status naming that must be resolved before implementing the account-status check — see contradictions_resolved and escalations.","dom

content.thinking:
Let me analyze the inputs from Bird and MJ for consistency before producing a handoff brief.

Bird says "suspended account" → MJ says "locked" account. These are different terms for what appears to be the same concept. This is a terminology conflict.

Bird says "session" → MJ says "token". This is another terminology mismatch, but this one is more benign — tokens implement sessions, so this is a domain-to-technical mapping that's expected.

The "suspended" vs "locked" one is a real terminology conflict that could cause bugs — Shaq might implement a check for "locked" status when the domain actually uses "suspended", or they could be genuinely different states.


I'm flagging this as a terminology conflict with confidence below 50%, which means I need to route back to Bird for alignment. But I can still produce the handoff brief — I just need to explicitly note the terminology mismatch in the contradictions_resolved section before moving forward with synthesis.

Bird should confirm 'suspended' as canonical and MJ should update the architecture doc."}],"confidence":{"level":45,"high_confidence_areas":["Session sliding window implementation via Redis TTL is well-aligned between Bird and MJ","AC1 (session expiry) is fully specified and implementable now","HTTP status codes (401 for expired session, 403 for blocked account) are agreed"],"low_confidence_areas":["Account status terminology — 'suspended' vs 'locked' is unresolved","Whether account suspension should retroactively kill active sessions","Whether other non-active account states exist beyond suspended/locked"],"assumptions":["Bird and MJ intended 'suspended' and 'locked' to mean the same thing (naming drift, not a real domain distinction)","The 30-minute window is always sliding (reset on activity), never fixed from login time","Redis is already available in the deployment environment"]}}
